entry.title: Re: How to create connection for Presto simba driver
entry.content:
Download and install the Simba ODBC driver for Google BigQuery, and configure the driver based on your Windows or Linux platform.
Find driver downloads for the Magnitude Simba driver for BigQuery and access to documentation on the Google Cloud website.

Select the link Windows 64-bit (msi) or Linux 32-bit and 64-bit (tar.gz) to start the installation.
After you install the driver, follow the instructions to configure the driver for either a data source name (DSN) connection or a server name (DSN-less) connection. Then create the Google BigQuery ODBC datastore.

You can check the documentation below for more information:

  • Download and install the Simba ODBC driver
  • Download and install the Simba ODBC driver - Google BigQuery

entry.title: Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the No-Code Playground
entry.content:
Introduction
In this post, I’ll walk through a simple way to try SAP RPT-1 with your own SAP Transportation Management ™ data—no model training required, no code needed. We’ll use the free no-code playground at https://rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard to predict a carrier based on historical freight order data. In the next blog, I’ll show how to achieve the same using the SAP AI SDK and how to trigger it from an S/4HANA ABAP environment as a process controller strategy for TM.

What is SAP RPT-1?

SAP-RPT-1 is a relational pretrained transformer for use on relational and structured data. It's developed and maintained by SAP.

Relational Foundation Models (RFMs) are large-scale machine learning models designed to understand, process, and do predictions on tabular and relational data.

SAP-RPT-1 is a table-native model aiming to achieve highest prediction quality and lowest error rates for predictions on tabular business data. It's pretrained, and doesn't need additional training or fine-tuning steps

It uses in-context learning, allowing you to provide data records and get instant, reliable predictions without training a model. Unlike traditional ML workflows that require training, de-biasing, and specialized expertise, RPT-1 is a generic relational model that can perform prediction and forecasting on tabular data with just an API call—or via the no-code playground.

How to try RPT-1
You can experience RPT-1 in two ways:

  • No-code testing playground: https://rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard
  • SAP AI SDK (covered in my next blog)

Use case: Predicting the carrier for a Freight Order
There are many TM scenarios for RPT-1. To keep things simple, we’ll predict the carrier for a freight order based on historical data. Other examples you might explore:

  • Predicting arrival date/time
  • Predicting potential issues with a carrier invoice based on freight order change history

Step-by-step: Testing RPT-1 with your data (no code)

  1. Prepare your data
  • From the TM worklist, download a list of Freight Orders as an MS Excel file.
  • Identify the column you want RPT-1 to predict (for example, Carrier). Add the tag [PREDICT] to that required cell for this column.
  • Save the file as a .csv.
  1. Use the no-code playground
  • Go to https://rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard and sign in.
  • Upload your CSV file.
  • The rows/column requiring prediction will be highlighted based on the [PREDICT] tag.

  •  

  1. Generate predictions
  • Click the Predict button.
  • Review the predicted values for the [PREDICT] column directly in the interface.

  •  

That’s it—no model training, no feature engineering. You can iterate quickly by refining your dataset, adjusting which column is tagged [PREDICT], and re-uploading.

Conclusion and next steps
This walkthrough shows how quickly you can validate an SAP TM use case with RPT-1 using your own data—without training or managing models, even for larger datasets. In my next blog, I’ll demonstrate how to consume RPT-1 using the SAP AI SDK and trigger it from an S/4HANA system in an ABAP environment as part of a process controller strategy for TM.

#RPT1 #RPT-1 #SAPTM

entry.title: Re: Replicate 4 years if data from SAP HANA SYSTEM 1 to S...
entry.content: Partial data replication in SAP HANA allows selective data transfer rather than full system mirroring, using methods like SAP Landscape Transformation (SLT) Replication Server, SAP Data Services (BODS), or Smart Data Integration (SDI). It enables filtering, selecting specific tables, or using SQL queries to subset data for improved performance and reduced storage, commonly using triggers for real-time updates.

entry.title: Re: Try Vibing Steampunk to generate ABAP; setting up VSP with a CAL instance
entry.content:
I have connected my own small MCP with tools to read code, search, read transports and diffs. Then I created a set of agents with skills for reviewing and documentation, I added all our naming conventions/coding guidelines and also some other documentation into it's knowledge base.  

As a final touch I connected to Atlassian MCP to connect to our Jira/Confluence. The combination of reading our real code, our guidelines and architectural documentation plus access to JIRA and confluence is amazingly powerful. It does actual code reviews not only checking the code quality, but comparing it to our guidelines, checking against the user stories and specs and it even knows about our system landscape, and can suggest alternative ways to accomplish things.

I have to try this, so I don't have to maintain my own MCP server.

entry.title: Re: Sales Order Print Output Log S4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Dear @Tisha_Haria,

SAP does not currently publish a released standard API that returns a sales order confirmation PDF/xstring directly from the sales order number. In the released APIs for Sales, SAP explicitly documents Billing Document Read, Cancel, GetPDF but there is no equivalent released GetPDF operation for sales orders / sales order confirmations

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/3bac8c01d7024ae1b15cf848ce12544e.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/10ec8998a151440795e797cb098321bb.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/990f1ce2078f42e4b593d6d060966bd8.html

Summary:

Use the output request layer to establish the relation to the sales order and use API_CLOUD_PRINT_PULL_SRV only for the final document pull. In practice, that means exposing C_OutputRequestItemDEX through a custom CDS / external API and using the output request layer to identify the relevant sales document output. SAP explicitly supports exposing custom CDS views as external APIs and states that customers can build their own CDS views based on released CDS views because SAP does not appear to publish another released standard API that directly provides the missing sales order-to-print-queue-item link for sales order confirmation PDFs in Public Cloud. The most supportable standard design is to use C_OutputRequestItemDEX as the output request correlation layer and API_CLOUD_PRINT_PULL_SRV for the PDF retrieval itself

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/f4dcfd6e14b54177b98d6099ddb2014e.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/3ccb50e724b045508fea8b2cf1774b2b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/b0000072fe994d7b9d5e015adf066059.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/e8c6621864654025997c9daea001a778/1d11db7eb99b4c6f8a9c3ba3de939c3d.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Multiple WH Task from HU in GR Area
entry.content:
Dear @bastianD,

In Public Cloud handling units are used across logistics processes, including inventory management and warehouse management. As long as the stock remains packed in the original mixed handling unit the follow-on stock transfer remains HU-based because SAP documents the stock transfer of HU-managed stock by assigning the handling units to a stock transfer order. If separate stock transfer orders are required per product the standard workaround is to first change the HU structure in Inventory Management by unpacking the HU or in Warehouse Management by repacking/splitting it for example by splitting one pallet into two pallets or moving one product to another pallet. After that each material can be processed separately in the follow-on flow

Example: If inbound HU 100 contains material A and material B and the target is STO 1 for A and STO 2 for B the standard workaround is:

  1. post GR for the mixed HU,
  2. unpack the HU or repack/split it in the warehouse,
  3. separate the stock physically/system-wise per material,
  4. then process each material in its own follow-on transfer

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/11fae0d93efe425cbead100510ab4b3c.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/5ae9c353b677b44ce10000000a174cb4.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/deb44d96aa234bada7d5893f1802ab63.html

https://help.sap.com/doc/7c9e0bbbd1664c2581b2038a1c7ae4b3/2602.500/en-US/FSD_CE2602.pdf

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Group Journal Entries report with complete user name
entry.content:
Dear @belen_fernandez2,

In standard Public Cloud Group Reporting there is no delivered app that shows the group journal entry list together with the user’s full business name in the same standard output. SAP documents the standard Group Reporting journal entry apps as Display Group Journal Entries and Display Group Journal Entries With Reporting Logic and the released Group Reporting data model exposes the user-related field as USERID in objects such as I_CnsldtnGroupJrnlEntrItmDEX

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/042c2221ad3242ffab7400d48a1081e5.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/b5cad442ec224b278aef4eedf4cc2b26.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/e8b11269736d47dbb04fa7a4694f03e6.html

So the clean Public Cloud approach is to build a custom report on top of the released Group Reporting source and enrich it with standard business user master data. SAP Public Cloud VDM catalog includes Business User Basic and SAP documents I_BusinessUserBasic with fields such as UserID, FirstName and LastName. If a single full-name field is required, SAP also provides the standard Business User Read service (QUERYBUSINESSUSERIN), which returns the person’s full name

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/8573b810511948c8a99c0672abc159aa.html

https://api.sap.com/cdsviews/I_BUSINESSUSERBASIC

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f1531d40f450474dbf95f0404cb62007/d2fb90305084421e883978b4bf9da775.html

Summary:

A practical design would be to use I_CnsldtnGrpJrnlItemC (Group Data Analysis Cube) or I_ConsolidationJournalEntryC as the journal entry source, join the Group Reporting USERID to I_BusinessUserBasic.UserID and then expose the journal entry fields together with FirstName and LastName in the custom output. From a business perspective this means the report can show for example the group journal entry number, posting data and the responsible user instead of only the technical user ID

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/b5cad442ec224b278aef4eedf4cc2b26.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/e8b11269736d47dbb04fa7a4694f03e6.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/26238f3caa954f3c83ccff79f959b1c1.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/8573b810511948c8a99c0672abc159aa.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

SAP Business AI is transforming how organizations drive efficiency and innovation. But how do you activate these capabilities and ensure their effective usage? Join us for a 45-minute live session on April 14, 2026, at 6:00 AM (America/Vancouver) to learn everything you need to know.

entry.title: Re: Networking Meetup Dublin - March
entry.content:
Really appreciate the efforts of Alan for starting this initiative in Dublin and bringing together professionals and learners in the SAP space.

Attended the SAP Community Networking Meetup in Dublin today, and it was a highly engaging and insightful session. The discussions primarily focused on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and its growing importance in building scalable and intelligent enterprise solutions. A key highlight was understanding how VAT tax solutions can be developed using SAP technologies and how such implementations can bring efficiency, accuracy and compliance benefits to businesses.

We also talked about how AI capabilities are increasingly being integrated within the SAP ecosystem, making solutions more intelligent and future-ready. These conversations provided valuable perspectives on how SAP continues to evolve with emerging technologies.

I would strongly encourage SAP professionals , students and who are interested in learning and exploring SAP to join future meetups. It’s a great platform to learn, network and gain real-world insights that you won’t find in classrooms or online courses.

Looking forward to attending more such sessions and continuing to be part of this growing SAP community in Dublin.

entry.title: Re: How to Create a Define View in SAP CDS
entry.content:
Rather confusing post... "define view" is the command that, well, literally does what it says: starts the definition of a CDS view. It's like an ABAP report starts with REPORT. But while it would be accurate to say "create a report", "create a define view" is just not a thing. One could "create a CDS view" or "define a CDS view". Makes me wonder if author even understands the subject...

There are numerous blog posts, tutorials, YT videos, etc. on how to create a CDS view. This SAP Learning course fragment, for example, provides detailed and accurate information, as well as step-by-step instructions with good quality screenshots:
https://learning.sap.com/courses/building-data-models-with-the-abap-dictionary-and-abap-core-data-services/defining-a-basic-cds-view_b94f4075-778b-4311-b28b-f9f46b870c5e

There is also this tutorial about classic CDS views that correctly points they're deprecated and view entity is the way to go (and also has a link to a new tutorial):
https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/abap-dev-adt-create-cds-view..html

All of this is very easily found on Google.

entry.title: Re: Does HANA Planviz from HANA Studio execute a temporar...
entry.content: Thank you for your reply. I deployed one audit policy and generated a planviz. The columns of AUDIT_LOG OBJECT_NAME and STATEMENT_STRING show it was calling the PLANVIZ_ACTION procedure. Yes, as you pointed out, I did not find out any document describing the details. From SAP internal documents, I found some relevant descriptions like "For executing a query with SQL Analyzer, two built-in procedures and one special statement are used. For turning on/off SQL Analyzer mode, compiling a query, saving the execution statistics into SQL Analyzer tables, PLANVIZ_ACTION and ADVANCED_PLANVIZ_ACTION built-in procedures are used. And for executing compiled query itself, a special statement, "EXECUTE PLANVIZ STATEMENT ID '{ID}'" is used." Hope it helps. Thanks.

entry.title: Re: CI MCP Server: Managing SAP Cloud Integration with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Act as a CPI runtime observability and architecture auditor.

Across the current tenant:

1. Runtime Analysis
- Identify the top 5 failing iFlows in the last 24 hours
- Classify failures by type: adapter, mapping, script, external system
- Highlight any recurring correlation IDs or patterns

2. Observability Gaps
- List iFlows with zero custom headers in recent executions
- Identify which ones process business-critical objects (orders, assets, etc.)
- Flag where traceability is effectively impossible

3. Design vs Runtime Drift
- Compare deployed runtime artifacts vs design-time artifacts
- Identify:
  - undeployed iFlows
  - multiple active versions
  - orphaned artifacts (never executed)

4. Integration Risk Assessment
For each high-traffic iFlow:
- No retry handling?
- No exception subprocess?
- No persistence / audit trail?
- Tight coupling to external endpoints?

5. Output
- Provide a ranked list of the 10 highest-risk integrations
- For each: root cause category, blast radius, and recommended fix (minimal, not theoretical)
- Recommend which 3 iFlows should be fixed this week to reduce 80% of operational noise
- Justify with data (failure frequency, lack of observability, business impact)

A fun prompt example 🙂

entry.title: Re: Different consolidation group in handling unit Outbou...
entry.content: Hi Bastian, Cross-delivery HU is a missing feature for public cloud warehouse management. Delivery items of one delivery have the same consolidation group but different deliveries have different consolidation groups. So mixing different consolidation groups in one HU is not supported yet. Best Regards, Clark

entry.title: Re: Order Generation Behavior in MPLAN After Restarting R...
entry.content:
Hi,

Greetings.

To answer your questions:

1. There will be no orders created with the new scheduling for the cut-over period.

2. System will continue from the date of scheduling start date.

3. If orders were created for the cut-over period with previous scheduling, they all need to be closed, as you have checked the "completion Confirmation" .

Hope this helps.

entry.title: Material ledger value change is 0
entry.content:
Hi All,

We tried to perform a movement type 905 GD returns QI - return goods to plant by MIGO. We have created 2 material documents for two plants. the first one is perform as expect which will increase the inventory and  credit the COGSreturn. and the material document also seems normal that the quantity change and value change is a positive value.

 

And  the second movement, there is 0 amount in the FI document and also 0 amount in the material ledger value change, only affect the quantity in the material ledger.

Supposed two documents have the same parameters, the only difference is the plant. The material is moving average material. The second plant is 0 inventory in the begining, so I am not sure whether is it related. Anyone can help to answer? I checked the OMJJ config, seems it is not related, all the parameter are the same except plant.

entry.title: Re: When executing FBL1N, the user data in the document h...
entry.content:
Why it happens is because, in Traditional Chinese mode, the system sometimes fails to render the document header text field correctly on the initial execution. When you re-select the layout, SAP refreshes the ALV grid and reloads the field catalog, which forces the correct display of user data. In English mode, the field catalog loads correctly the first time, so the issue does not appear.

Having said that, this is not a data issue but a UI rendering problem tied to language-dependent layouts. Re-saving the layout in Traditional Chinese or applying the latest SAP Notes usually fixes it.

entry.title: Re: SAP MDG News: AI Use Cases delivered, demoed and presented
entry.content: This is amazing stuff. Thanks for sharing details.

entry.title: Re: Material ledger value change is 0
entry.content:
Why the first plant worked is because, the first plant had existing inventory and a moving average price. When you posted the 905 movement type (returns to plant), SAP could calculate a value based on the MAP. That value flowed into both the FI document (credit COGS return) and the material ledger value change.

Why the second plant shows zero value is because, in the second plant, the material had zero inventory at the beginning. With MAP valuation, SAP cannot derive a value if there is no stock and no MAP maintained. As a result, the system posts only the quantity change (to update stock levels) but no value change in FI or material ledger. This is expected behavior. MAP requires existing stock or a prior valuation to calculate a value. If none exists, the posting is quantity-only.  

In short,  the difference is not plant config, but the fact that the second plant had zero stock and no MAP value, so SAP posted only quantity without value.

entry.title: Re: Is there a released BADi to modify item-level tax cod...
entry.content: To my knowledge, there is no officially released BAdI in SAP that allows direct modification of the item-level tax code (MWSKZ) during MIRO before posting. The standard BAdIs you checked (MRM_CHECK, MRM_MRIS_IDAT_MODIFY, MRM_ITEM_CUSTFIELDS, MRM_HEADER_CHECK) do not provide safe hooks for changing tax codes. The recommended approach is to use the enhancement framework around invoice verification, specifically BADI_MRM_ITEM or EXIT_SAPLMRMP_001 (user exit), depending on your release

entry.title: Re: Billing Condition Type to GL Account CDS view for cre...
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, building a Sales Register report that ties billing document items, condition types, and the GL accounts used for postings is tricky because the released CDS views do not expose all the joins you need directly. The challenge you are hitting is that the accounting document linkage (GL account) is not straightforwardly available in the pricing element CDS.

Try joining I_BillingDocumentItem and I_OperationalAcctgDocItem via SourceDocumentItemUUID. That is the key that ensures the FI posting lines (GL accounts) are correctly tied to the billing item. Then enrich with condition type from the pricing element view.

entry.title: Re: SAP CPI - json value characater gets escaped when con...
entry.content:
If you are using standard JSON to XML converter, then it will escape characters like < or & to keep the xml valid. To undo this I'm using groovy script right after the converter to replace them to raw form. 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;

def Message processData(Message message) {
def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
body = body.replaceAll("<", "<").replaceAll("&", "&")
message.setBody(body)
return message;
}

entry.title: Re: How to force measurement point entry in PM order conf...
entry.content: No standard way to force measurement entry during PM order confirmation or completion. You can encourage it via status profiles or completion checks, but enforcement requires a custom enhancement (BAdI/user exit). This is why you have not found a delivered option; it is not overlooked, it is by design.

entry.title: What is variant configuration and when should it be used?
entry.content:
If you’ve ever watched a sales rep, an engineer, and a production planner debate the “right” version of a product like it’s the final round of a quiz show, this post is for you. We’re about to demystify SAP Advanced Variant Configuration—the not‑so‑secret weapon that turns a jungle of options into a calm, reliable ordering and manufacturing flow. Grab your coffee (configured to taste), and let’s make sense of complexity without making your master data cry.

 

What is Variant Configuration (and When Should You Use It)?

If you’ve ever tried to order coffee with more than three modifiers—extra hot, half-caf, oat milk, two pumps hazelnut—you already understand product complexity. Now imagine doing that with forklifts, machinery, or electronics and trying to keep your master data from bursting into tears. That’s where SAP Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) comes in: it’s the difference between a chaotic spreadsheet of doom and a tidy, rules-driven model that says, “Don’t worry, I’ve got this.”

 

The Big Idea (No Bullet Points, Promise)

Advanced Variant Configuration lets you represent countless product possibilities using one configurable material. Instead of creating a separate material for every “diesel + 45 performance + 3‑ton + blue + camera” combo, you define characteristics (like engine type, performance, tonnage, color, and accessories), group them logically, and wire them together with rules that keep things sensible. You also build a Super BOM that contains all potential components and a Super Routing with all potential operations. The system then does the heavy lifting: given a customer’s selection, it picks the right parts and steps automatically. You keep your master data lean, your sales team fast, and your production team off your back.

How It Works (In Real Life)

It starts with modeling. You create a configurable material, define the characteristics your customers care about, and organize them into a class made for configuration. You then build one master bill of materials—the Super BOM—that includes every component any variant might need, and a routing that covers all possible operations. Finally, you add object dependencies—rules that clarify what goes with what, so “electric + 55 performance + 5‑ton” doesn’t sneak past quality like a cat through an open door.

When a sales order comes in, the salesperson selects the values the customer wants. SAP looks at those choices, applies the rules, explodes the Super BOM, and produces a variant-specific structure and routing that production can actually build. No guesswork. No “which one did we mean?” Slack threads. Just the right components, every time.

A Forklift Walks Into a Showroom…

Picture a forklift product line with three drive types (diesel, gas, electric), multiple performance levels, tonnage from one to five tons, several colors, and a menu of accessories. The number of combinations balloons fast—into the tens of thousands if you let it. Without AVC, you’d need a material for each variant, plus a BOM and routing for each. Congratulations, you now manage a small library of nearly identical records that differ on line ten, footnote two. With AVC, you model the product once, capture the logic once, and reuse it endlessly. You can even define commonly sold configurations as material variants for stocking or quick quoting, while still relying on the same brains behind the scenes.

When You Should (Absolutely) Use Advanced Variant Configuration

Use AVC when your products are like LEGO sets—modular, customizable, and prone to enthusiastic customers wanting “just one more piece.” If your sales process involves choosing from a menu of features, if your master data threatens to spread like ivy, or if you need to introduce new options without rebuilding everything from scratch, AVC will save your roadmap (and your weekends). It’s especially handy in configure-to-order and engineer-to-order environments, where enforcing valid combinations isn’t a nice-to-have; it’s the difference between smooth production and emergency meetings.

Why Teams End Up Loving It

First, there’s the master data diet: one configurable material instead of thousands. Maintenance becomes manageable, and the risk of copy‑paste errors drops dramatically. Sales gets faster because the system helps them choose valid, buildable options without phoning a friend in engineering. Production gets exactly what was promised, complete with the right components and operations. And finance appreciates that configuration rules can gently steer choices toward cost‑appropriate combinations. In short, AVC keeps everyone aligned, which, in most companies, is rarer than a quiet inbox.

What You Need in Place

Success with AVC starts with a clear, honest picture of your product. That means well-defined characteristics that reflect how you actually sell and build, not how someone wished you did two reorgs ago. Your Super BOM and Super Routing should be comprehensive and well-structured, so dependencies can do their job cleanly. Each configurable material needs a configuration profile to control behavior, and your team should be comfortable with the Product Modeling Environment (PME/PMEVC) to maintain logic. Finally, make sure your modelers speak the language of dependencies—preconditions, selection conditions, procedures, and constraints—because that’s how your product rules come alive. If you’re moving to Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC), expect better performance and more modeling power with the same core principles—like upgrading from a trusty bike to an e‑bike that still pedals, but flies uphill.

The Payoff (and a Gentle Nudge)

Advanced Variant Configuration turns option overload into a controlled, elegant model. It keeps your master data slim, your configurations valid, and your lead times realistic. If your product line is already complex—or your roadmap suggests it soon will be—AVC is the practical way to offer customers choice without breeding chaos. A great way to start is to pick one product line, define the must-have characteristics, draft a Super BOM that mirrors reality, and pilot it with a few real orders. Iterate, tighten the rules, and watch the noise go down while speed and confidence go up.

 

If you’ve made it this far, congrats—you now speak fluent “configurable product.” The next best step is small and practical: pick one product line, sketch the must‑have characteristics, and let a pilot model prove the value. From there, you’ll refine the rules, trim the noise, and watch quoting and production get measurably smoother. May your dependencies be tidy, your BOMs be super, and your lead times politely short

entry.title: Incomplete date handling - are incomplete dates converted to nulls when using CSVs via ODBC?
entry.content:
Hello,

I am having an issue where incomplete dates, e.g. "2025" are evaluated as nulls in formulas in Crystal. I am using CSV files accessed via ODBC as the data source as it's the only available method with my software. When I check for nulls on the date field, it comes up as null on incomplete dates. If I enable "Convert database nulls to default" and format it using:
Totext({table_csv.datefield}, "yyyy-MM-dd")
It returns -4714-12-31.

I've been trying to understand why this is occurring and have seen semi-related posts online that seem to indicate this issue is caused by the ODBC driver being unable to parse incomplete dates, so Crystal sees them as nulls. Is anyone able to confirm if this is correct?

I think my solution will be to force the dates to strings on export from the database, but would be good to understand this potential limitation.

Thanks

entry.title: Re: Seeking advice: SAP Generative AI Developer - Hands-on access and Python prerequisites
entry.content:
Hi,

We are glad that you find the learn journey useful. Let me answer your questions one by one:

  1. The learning journey includes a hands on practice  system setup specifically for this learning  journey and the certification, Hands-on Practice for Solving your business problems using prompts and LLMs in SAP's Generative AI Hub. You don't need to setup the BTP Global Enterprise account just to practice the exercises in this learning journey. You can do that if you want to experiment with more models, or develop a pilot and working generative AI solution.
  2. No, there is no Python course by SAP.  However, code in this learning journey is presented in a simple manner with explanations. The focus is on problem solving skills instead of coding knowledge in a particular language. I wish you all the best for the learning  journey.

entry.title: Connecting custom Joule Agents to MCP servers: A POC Architecture for Enterprise HR Intelligence
entry.content:
Building a Custom Joule Agent with MCP on SAP BTP

Most enterprise AI initiatives struggle not because of models, but because of data fragmentation and lack of controlled access across systems. The real challenge is not "chat with your data." It is enabling AI systems to reason across multiple systems — safely, explainably, and in a governed way.

In this blog, I walk through a reference architecture for a POC where a custom Joule agent created with Joule Studio integrates:

  • Curated HR data stored in HANA Cloud
  • Talent management data stored in an external system
  • A governed Model Context Protocol (MCP) server layer
  • A custom query service
  • SAP BTP environment (Cloud Foundry)

The Use Case

Imagine yourself as the VP of Engineering at a large software company. The company is undertaking a major cloud migration project. As the VP, you need to understand the skill landscape of your organization to make informed decisions about hiring and upskilling initiatives.

Today, answering these questions would typically require multiple teams, manual data extraction, and several days to weeks of turnaround time. Skills data lives in an external system. Core employee data lives in SuccessFactors. Insights require joins across both, and responses must be grounded and explainable.

This is where a custom Joule agent + an MCP server becomes powerful.

Concretely, the VP needs to ask questions like:

  • "Which engineers in the Product Development team have Kubernetes experience backed by internal project evidence?"
  • "Show the existing skill gaps we have for the cloud migration project."
  • "Recommend 5 engineers suitable for leading the cloud migration project, and who has related skills and can potentially be upskilled."

These questions are complex. They require data from the external talent management system integrated with master HR data, some level of reasoning, and domain knowledge about organizational structure. Standard Joule capabilities will not handle this effectively, as they require knowledge of the external system's data model. 

The Demo


The Solution: Architecture Overview

Figure 1 — High-level architecture: Joule Studio → MCP Server → Query App → Data Sources


Data Layer

Figure 2 — Data layer: synthetic HR data in HANA Cloud, talent/skills data in PostgreSQL

Since we did not have access to a SuccessFactors instance, we created a data generation library that produced realistic HR data in the style of SuccessFactors with clear reporting structures, departments, roles, and organizational assignments for each employee in a fictional organization. The generated data was then imported into HANA Cloud and consisted of 8 relational tables.

For the external talent management data, another library was created to synthesize employee skills data. The generated skills data was linked to the master HR data from the previous step to ensure downstream integration. A PostgreSQL instance was created on AWS EC2, replicating the external system scenario.

Why synthetic data design matters

Synthetic data must be structurally believable. If relationships, distributions, and cardinalities are unrealistic, LLM reasoning becomes trivial and the demo loses credibility. Our generated data includes:

  • Skill frequency skewed to realistic distributions
  • Proficiency levels assigned probabilistically
  • Evidence count tied to proficiency
  • Full referential integrity across HR and talent tables

This ensures the LLM encounters realistic ambiguity rather than artificially clean data. When the agent searches for cloud migration candidates, it faces partial matches, missing evidence, and competing skill profiles — just as it would in a real enterprise.


Query App Layer

Instead of letting the LLM write raw SQL against HANA Cloud and PostgreSQL, we introduced a query application layer that sits between the MCP server and the databases. This is one of the most important architectural decisions in the system.

Why a query app layer?

  • Prevents unsafe or malformed SQL from reaching production databases
  • Encapsulates cross-system joins in well-tested logic
  • Controls the shape and size of responses returned to the LLM
  • Improves reliability and makes tool behaviour deterministic

The query app exposes predefined operations as a FastAPI service. Each operation maps to a specific business question:

OperationPurpose
get_employee_skills(employee_id)Individual skill profile with proficiency and evidence
get_org_skill_summary(org_unit)Org-wide aggregation of skills and gaps
find_candidates_for_project(skill_set, min_proficiency)Multi-criteria candidate search
get_skill_gap_analysis(program_id)Gap analysis against a target skill profile
get_evidence_inventory(employee_id, skill_id)Supporting documentation for a specific skill claim

Example: Multi-Skill AND Search (Relational Division)

One of the more interesting queries demonstrates why the query layer exists. When the VP asks for engineers with both Kubernetes AND SAP integration experience, the system must enforce AND semantics — employees who have all specified skills, not just any one:

SELECT es.employee_id
FROM employee_skill es
WHERE es.skill_id = ANY($1) AND es.proficiency >= $2
GROUP BY es.employee_id
HAVING count(DISTINCT es.skill_id) = $3

The HAVING clause is the key: $3 equals the total number of requested skills. An employee only passes if they have every requested skill at sufficient proficiency. Without it, this would be an OR search. This is not something an LLM should be constructing on the fly against a production database.

Example: Org Hierarchy Traversal (Recursive CTE)

When the VP asks about the Engineering organization, the query must include all descendant org units — not just the top-level node:

 
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
    SELECT org_id FROM org_unit_ref WHERE org_id = $1
    UNION ALL
    SELECT o.org_id FROM org_unit_ref o
    JOIN org_tree t ON o.parent_org_id = t.org_id
)

This walks the parent_org_id chain, collecting all descendant orgs in a single query. The resulting set is then joined to employee_skill for aggregation. These queries are tested, parameterized, and isolated in dedicated query modules — the LLM never sees a table name or connection string.


Designing the MCP Server

You can think of MCP as a contract layer that translates business-safe operations into LLM-accessible tools. The MCP server provides the interface between the Joule agent and the backend systems:

  • Advertises available tools to the LLM
  • Optionally exposes prompt templates for structured queries
  • Provides resources (contextual data the LLM can read)
  • Supplies high-level instructions to guide the LLM's reasoning
  • Listens for MCP client connections from the Joule agent

We used FastMCP (Python) to build the server. The server's instructions provide the cognitive framework for the LLM:

 
mcp = FastMCP(
    "tm-skills",
    instructions=(
        "You have access to a Talent Management Skills API that stores employee "
        "skill profiles, proficiency scores, evidence, and org hierarchy data. "
        "Use the tools to answer HR and talent questions. Employee IDs look like "
        "EMP000001. Org IDs look like ORG030. Skill IDs are numeric (e.g. 1). "
        "Start by browsing the skill catalog (browse_skills) if you need to find "
        "skill IDs by name."
    ),
    host=settings.host,
    port=settings.port,
    lifespan=audit_lifespan,
)

Tools are thin wrappers around the query app's API. Each tool is roughly 5 lines of actual logic:

Example: MCP Tool Definition

 
@mcp.tool()
@audited
async def get_top_experts(
    skill_id: int, min_proficiency: int = 4, limit: int = 10) -> dict:
    """Find the top experts for a specific skill, ranked by proficiency."""
    response = await client.get(
        f"{BASE_URL}/tm/skills/{skill_id}/experts",
        params={"min_proficiency": min_proficiency, "limit": limit},
        headers={"X-API-Key": API_KEY}
    )
    return response.json()

The @audited decorator captures timing, parameters, and success/failure for every invocation. The full server exposes 16 tools, 2 resources, and 3 prompt templates:

MCP toolBackend OperationPurpose
get_employee_skillsQuery App → HANA + PostgresIndividual skill profile
get_top_skillsQuery App → PostgresIdentify strongest competencies
get_org_skill_summaryQuery App → HANA + PostgresOrg-wide skill aggregation
find_candidates_for_projectQuery App → HANA + PostgresMulti-criteria candidate search
get_evidence_inventoryQuery App → PostgresSupporting documentation
browse_skillsQuery App → PostgresBrowse and search the skill catalog

Why MCP instead of direct Joule skills?

At first glance, it may seem simpler to implement these capabilities directly as Joule skills. However, this quickly becomes limiting in enterprise scenarios. MCP provides:

  • Structured tool discovery — the LLM can inspect available tools at runtime
  • Context resources — the database schema and business questions catalog are surfaced alongside tools
  • Prompt templates — standardised input patterns improve multi-step reasoning consistency
  • Decoupled architecture — the MCP server can be versioned and deployed independently of Joule
  • Observability — tool calls can be intercepted, logged, and audited at the protocol level

MCP transforms a set of API calls into an agentic contract between the LLM and the enterprise backend.


Connecting MCP to Joule Studio

Once the MCP server was deployed to Cloud Foundry, connecting it to Joule Studio involved the following steps:

  1. Deploy the MCP server application to Cloud Foundry (separate subaccount)
  2. Create a Destination in the Joule Studio subaccount pointing to the MCP server URL
  3. Configure authentication on the destination (API key injected via URL.headers)
  4. Register the MCP server as a tool provider in the agent configuration
  5. Define agent instructions that guide the LLM on when and how to use each tool

This effectively turns Joule into an MCP client, capable of discovering and invoking external tools through the standard protocol.

MCP Server Entry Point

The MCP protocol endpoint and REST audit endpoints coexist on the same ASGI process:

 
app = mcp.streamable_http_app()
app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=settings.cors_origins.split(","), ...)
uvicorn.run(app, host=settings.host, port=settings.port)

Why Cloud Foundry?

  • Decouples the MCP runtime from Joule's own lifecycle
  • Allows independent scaling and service bindings
  • Aligns with existing BTP enterprise deployment patterns

Kyma would also be a valid choice, especially for event-driven or microservice-heavy designs. For a tool-centric MCP service, Cloud Foundry is well-suited.


Observability: The Differentiator

One of the most valuable aspects of this implementation is the observability layer. This is where the system moves beyond a demo and into enterprise territory.

We built a custom MCP audit dashboard that provides real-time visibility into the agent's behaviour:

  • Which tools were selected and in what order
  • Chained call sequences (multi-step reasoning traces)
  • Parameters passed to each tool
  • Response payload sizes and latency
  • Gaps between calls representing LLM reasoning time (typically 2–5 seconds)

Audit Schema

Every tool invocation is recorded in a local SQLite database using WAL mode for concurrent read/write:

 
CREATE TABLE tool_calls (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    timestamp       TEXT    NOT NULL,
    session_id      TEXT,
    tool_name       TEXT    NOT NULL,
    parameters      TEXT,        -- JSON-serialized
    success         INTEGER NOT NULL,
    duration_ms     REAL    NOT NULL
);

Figure 3 — MCP audit dashboard: real-time tool call traces with timing and parameter visibility

AI without observability is a black box. An agent with a tool audit trail is governed intelligence. Stakeholders can see exactly how the agent arrived at an answer, which builds trust and supports compliance requirements.


Security Model

Current implementation

The current setup is intentionally simplified for a POC, but the architecture is designed to evolve into a production-grade security model:

  • API key authentication between Joule (via BTP Destination) and the MCP server
  • API key authentication from MCP server to the query app
  • Separate subaccounts to enforce network isolation

Enterprise upgrade path

Moving this to production-grade security would involve:

  1. Replace API key auth with OAuth2 via XSUAA with principal propagation
  2. Add role-based access control at the tool level (e.g., HR Manager vs. VP-level queries)
  3. Enforce scoped destinations with service-to-service trust
  4. Log all tool invocations to the SAP Audit Log service
  5. Implement mTLS between the MCP server and the query app

Each upgrade can be applied incrementally without rearchitecting the solution. The separation of concerns — Joule → MCP → Query App → Database — means security layers slot in cleanly at each boundary.


Demo Walkthrough

To illustrate the end-to-end flow, here is what happens when the VP asks:

"Find 5 engineers in the Product Development team with Kubernetes and SAP integration experience backed by evidence."
  1. The LLM analyses the question and identifies the need for organizational data and skill data
  2. It calls browse_skills to resolve "Kubernetes" and "SAP integration" to skill IDs from the catalog
  3. It calls get_org_skill_summary to understand the Product Development team's skill landscape
  4. It calls find_candidates_for_project with the target skills and minimum proficiency thresholds
  5. It calls get_evidence_inventory for each shortlisted candidate to retrieve supporting project documentation
  6. The LLM synthesizes a ranked recommendation with proficiency levels, evidence, and organizational context

Figure 4 — End-to-end demo flow: from VP question to ranked candidate recommendation with evidence

This entire sequence happens in seconds, without manual intervention. The final response includes ranked candidates, skill proficiency levels, supporting project evidence, and organizational context. This is not retrieval. This is multi-step reasoning across multiple systems, governed by a controlled tool framework.


Lessons Learned

  1. Controlled query layers outperform free-form LLM SQL

    Encapsulating business logic in a query app layer dramatically improves reliability. The LLM does not need to understand database schemas, and results are consistent and predictable.

  2. Tool granularity shapes reasoning quality

    Tools that are too coarse limit the LLM's reasoning capability. Tools that are too fine-grained lead to excessive chaining and higher latency. Finding the right granularity requires iterating with realistic questions from your target persona.

  3. MCP instructions are the most important code you write

    The instructions provided to the MCP server have a disproportionate impact on how well the LLM selects and chains tools. Clear guidance on data formats, ID conventions, and recommended starting points (e.g., browse the skill catalog first) meaningfully improved response quality.

  4. Tool descriptions drive autonomous selection

    The LLM chooses tools based entirely on their descriptions. Vague descriptions lead to wrong tool choices. Specific, action-oriented descriptions with examples of when to use each tool dramatically improve selection accuracy. The word "verified" in a user's question should trigger get_evidence_backed_candidates, not get_top_experts — this only works if the descriptions are precise.

  5. Observability transforms stakeholder perception

    Showing the toolchain in real time during demos fundamentally changed the conversation. It shifted from "can I trust this AI?" to "how do we scale this?"

  6. Joule's 10KB response limit shapes MCP server design

    Joule Studio imposes a 10KB limit on individual tool responses. Any endpoint that returns large payloads (like a full skill catalog) needs pagination or filtering. This is a general principle for MCP servers: AI models work better with focused, bounded responses than with large data dumps.


When to Use This Pattern

This architecture is well-suited when:

  • Data spans SAP and non-SAP systems
  • Queries require joins or reasoning across system boundaries
  • Governance and auditability are requirements
  • You need explainable, evidence-backed answers
  • You want to decouple AI orchestration from business logic and data access


What's Next

Possible extensions to this reference architecture:

  • Replace synthetic HR data with live SuccessFactors data via OData
  • Add workflow actions such as project assignment recommendations that trigger approval flows
  • Integrate vector search for policy documents and unstructured knowledge bases
  • Add fine-grained, role-based authorization at the individual tool level
  • Deploy the MCP server to Kyma for event-driven architectures
  • Explore A2A (Agent-to-Agent) patterns for multi-domain orchestration


Final Thoughts

By combining Joule Studio with MCP servers, we can create AI agents that are grounded in real data, governed by structured tools, explainable through audit trails, and extensible across system boundaries.

MCP + Joule Studio is one of the first patterns in SAP BTP that makes this shift tangible. The patterns described here are not theoretical — they were built, tested, and demonstrated with a working POC.

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud Automatic Payment Approval and Payme...
entry.content: Hi Jeremy, can you please double check the solution you provided? I did not find the scope itme O77: Payment Management OFA: Cash Flow Management in https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/HomePage

entry.title: Re: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content:
Hi Soumya,
This is a rather complex requirement on FSCM.
I would suggest these steps:
1. You will need to activate credit check on order level in OVAK for all the order types where you want to do the credit check if there are Cement materials.
2. Then you need to control to trigger credit check if there are Cement materials in the order (Per your description you normally trigger check on deliveries, so I assume the order level check is only for the cement materials). You could do this with a No Check routine. This can be assigned in OVA8. You write a check on XVBAP table to lookup whether there are Cement materials. If NO, then set BYPASS-CREDIT_MANAGEMENT = X.
3. You also want to do the credit check with an overdue value different from what is stored on the credit check rule. For this I would suggest to create a copy of the credit check step in the UKM_CHECK_STEP implementation (UKM_CHECK_130) and maintain additional logic. You would need to change the standard check to use your 90 days value for certain scenarios. The credit check step is called in RFC, so the transaction code won't be simply available. You might need to come up with a different condition.
3*. An alternative would be to overwrite the logic where FSCM side determines the check rule to use another one (on which you maintain 90 days for check step 130). CHECK_ACCOUNT (CL_UKM_CREDIT_CHECKER)

Regards,
Edina

entry.title: SAP backup of data for a specific company code
entry.content:
There is a requirement to back up all SAP transactions on daily basis and keep the data physically in a specific country, including both manual and automatic entries.
Can the HANA database back up data for a specific company code? Or is there another way to achieve this?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAP backup of data for a specific company code
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Currently, there is no such a feature of taking backups for specific data in a table.
You need to extract the corresponding data from a table and back up the outputs at a safe external storage manually.

Please refer to 'EXPORT Statement (Data Import Export)' for details. 
For your information in case of importing the exported data, please also take a look at 'IMPORT Statement (Data Import Export)' in HANA SQL reference guide.

Best regards,
SAP Support
